---
title: The model
---

This page is the theoretical reference for the **tspaint** generative model: local
ancestry as a discrete character evolving up the branches of each marginal tree
of an inferred tree sequence. It fixes the notation, the state space, the
generator $Q$, the root frequencies $\pi$, the soft tip-emission noise model, and
the two Markov structures (vertical and horizontal) that the method keeps
separate. Inference (the blocked EM that fits these objects) is documented
elsewhere; here we describe only *what is being modelled*.

The construction reuses the **mugration** / discrete-phylogeography idea — a
discrete trait evolving under a continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) on a fixed
genealogy
([Lemey et al. (2009)](https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/journal.pcbi.1000520)) —
and computes likelihoods by Felsenstein pruning
([Felsenstein (1981)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF01734359)).
The novelty is not any single component but the synthesis: a generative
ancestry-CTMC run *per marginal tree* of an inferred tree sequence, with an
EM-learned generator and per-tip credibility, yielding a calibrated soft
posterior over local ancestry.

## Local ancestry as a character on the trees

An inferred tree sequence (from Relate, tsinfer, or SINGER) is a sequence of
**marginal trees**, one for each non-recombining genomic interval, sharing nodes
and edges across adjacent intervals. Every sampled haplotype is a **tip in every
marginal tree**. We treat the population of origin — the *ancestry* — as a
discrete character that sits at every node of every marginal tree and evolves *up*
each branch under a CTMC.

The two things being modelled, kept deliberately separate, are:

| Structure | Axis | Mechanism | What it estimates |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Vertical** | up a tree | ancestry CTMC with generator $Q$; Felsenstein pruning | the **ancestral-migration timescale** |
| **Horizontal** | along the genome | ancestry tracts, carried by **edge span** (recombination structure) | tract switching — **no free rate parameter** |

This decomposition — recombination (horizontal, a topology change) versus
ancestral migration (vertical, $Q$) — is the conceptual heart of the model and is
discussed in @sec-two-markov.

## State space and the generator $Q$

The ancestry state at a node is $s \in \{A, B\}$ for v1, generalising to
$s \in \{1, \dots, K\}$ for $K$ populations. Along a branch the state evolves as a
CTMC with **generator** (rate matrix) $Q$. For two states,

$$
Q = \begin{pmatrix} -q_{AB} & q_{AB} \\ q_{BA} & -q_{BA} \end{pmatrix},
$$ {#eq-generator}

where $q_{AB} \ge 0$ is the instantaneous $A \to B$ rate and $q_{BA} \ge 0$ the
$B \to A$ rate. For general $K$, $Q$ is a $K \times K$ matrix whose off-diagonal
entries are non-negative rates and whose **rows sum to zero**,

$$
\sum_{n} Q_{mn} = 0 \quad\text{for every } m, \qquad Q_{mn} \ge 0 \ (m \ne n),
$$ {#eq-rowsum}

so the diagonal is fixed by $Q_{mm} = -\sum_{n \ne m} Q_{mn}$. The implementation
enforces exactly these conditions
([`validate_generator`](../api/model.validate_generator.qmd) checks squareness,
@eq-rowsum, and non-negativity;
[`make_generator_2state`](../api/make_generator_2state.qmd) builds @eq-generator).
The code is written to be **generator-agnostic**: moving from two states to $K$ is
a generator swap, not a rewrite.

### Transition probabilities over a branch

The probability of ending in state $s'$ after time $t$ given start state $s$ is the
$(s, s')$ entry of the matrix exponential of $Q t$,

$$
P(t) = \exp(Q\,t), \qquad P(t)_{ss'} = \Pr(s' \text{ at child} \mid s \text{ at parent}),
$$ {#eq-transition}

computed by [`transition_matrix`](../api/model.transition_matrix.qmd). Because $Q$
has rows summing to zero, every row of $P(t)$ is a probability distribution for all
$t \ge 0$. Branch lengths $t$ come from the node times of the tree sequence; root
branches have length $0$ by tskit's definition and are handled through $\pi$ rather
than through @eq-transition.

### Root frequencies $\pi$

The state at the root of each marginal tree is drawn from the **root frequencies**
$\pi = (\pi_A, \pi_B)$ (more generally $\pi = (\pi_1, \dots, \pi_K)$, with
$\pi_m \ge 0$ and $\sum_m \pi_m = 1$). $\pi$ is a prior on the ancestry of the
grand-MRCA — an essentially *arbitrary* ancestral lineage — so a uniform $\pi$ is
the principled default. $\pi$ also appears inside the tip-emission noise model
(@sec-emission) as the fallback distribution.

$\pi$ is conceptually distinct from the generator's **stationary distribution**
$\pi^{\star}$, the equilibrium solving $\pi^{\star} Q = 0$
([`stationary_distribution`](../api/model.stationary_distribution.qmd)). The model
does not require $\pi = \pi^{\star}$; the root frequencies are a free prior, not
the CTMC equilibrium.

## Tip emission: a soft noise model with learned credibility {#sec-emission}

Reference haplotypes carry a sampled population **label** $\ell_i \in \{A, B\}$;
query haplotypes carry none. Rather than clamp a labelled tip to a one-hot
indicator, tspaint gives each tip a Felsenstein emission *likelihood vector* that is
a **noise model** parameterised by a per-tip **credibility** $w_i \in [0, 1]$:

$$
\mathrm{emission}_i(s) = w_i \,\mathbf{1}[s = \ell_i] + (1 - w_i)\,\pi(s)
\qquad \text{(labelled tip)},
$$ {#eq-emission}

$$
\mathrm{emission}_i(s) = \pi(s)
\qquad \text{(query tip; flat / root-frequency)}.
$$ {#eq-query-emission}

Equation @eq-emission is implemented by
[`tip_emission`](../api/model.tip_emission.qmd) (it forms $(1-w_i)\pi$ and adds
$w_i$ at the label index) and @eq-query-emission by
[`query_emission`](../api/model.query_emission.qmd). The credibility interpolates
between two limits:

| $w_i$ | Emission @eq-emission becomes | Meaning |
|:--|:--|:--|
| $w_i = 1$ | $\mathbf{1}[s = \ell_i]$ (one-hot) | **hard clamp** — the label is taken as truth |
| $0 < w_i < 1$ | a mix of label and $\pi$ | label believed, but the genealogy can override it |
| $w_i \to 0$ | $\pi(s)$ | tip effectively **re-inferred** from the rest of the tree (like a query) |

So a reference whose own down-pass posterior dissents from its label over a
genuinely foreign tract *is* the introgression signal — the same machinery, with
no separate code path. Reference and query tips are the **same kind of object**;
the only difference is the strength of the prior on the label.

### Anchors, the Beta prior, and why the panel must not all float {#sec-anchors}

The credibilities $\{w_i\}$ are learned, but they cannot all be free at once.
Letting the whole panel go soft simultaneously reintroduces unsupervised
degeneracy — label switching, a $Q$ / $w_i$ trade-off, and collapse. Two guards
are part of the model specification:

- **A trusted anchor set** is held at $w_i \equiv 1$ (the most-confidently
  unadmixed references), pinning the labelling so the rest can float against a
  fixed reference frame.
- The remaining soft references carry a $\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$ **prior with
  mass near $1$**, so a label is believed *unless the genealogy insists otherwise*.

Under this prior the credibility update has the MAP form

$$
w_i = \frac{\alpha - 1 + a_i}{\alpha + \beta - 2 + a_i + d_i},
$$ {#eq-credibility}

where $a_i$ and $d_i$ are the (span-weighted) expected counts of the tip's
posterior **agreeing** with and **disagreeing** with its label, accumulated during
inference. Equation @eq-credibility is shown here only to make the role of the
$\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$ prior concrete; how $a_i$ and $d_i$ are accumulated
belongs to the inference description.

## Why the state must be per-tip-per-tree {#sec-per-edge}

A haplotype is a tip in *every* marginal tree. If we kept **one ancestry colour per
individual** and pooled the evidence across all trees, the latent state would have
nowhere to vary along the genome and the estimate would converge to a **global
ancestry proportion** — the wrong target. Local ancestry requires the latent state
to be **per-tip-per-tree**, equivalently **per-edge**: free to differ at one locus
from the next.

Concretely, the latent ancestry lives on tree-sequence **edges**. An edge
$(\text{left}, \text{right}, \text{parent}, \text{child})$ carries a child lineage
over the half-open interval $[\text{left}, \text{right})$, and a clade that persists
across many marginal trees is a single set of edges with wide span (given a
persistence-preserving front end). This per-edge representation is also the channel
through which genome-scale autocorrelation enters: the same edge spanning many trees
ties the ancestry state together across that span, without a separate smoothing
parameter.

## The two Markov structures {#sec-two-markov}

The model contains two Markov processes on orthogonal axes, **both** explicit but
parameterised very differently.

### Vertical: the ancestry CTMC

Up any single tree, ancestry evolves under the generator $Q$ via the branch
transitions $P(t) = \exp(Q t)$ of @eq-transition. Felsenstein pruning over a
marginal tree turns these branch factors plus the tip emissions of @eq-emission
into node-level likelihoods and posteriors. The vertical process estimates only
the **ancestral-migration timescale** — how fast ancestry changes as one moves *up*
into the past — and nothing about tract switching along the chromosome.

### Horizontal: tracts carried by edge span, not by a rate $\rho$

Along the genome, contiguous stretches of one ancestry (**tracts**) are *not*
modelled with a free switch-rate parameter $\rho$ as in a copying/HMM model.
Instead, tract structure is **carried by edge span**. A lineage's ancestry can only
change where its **genealogical context** changes — at the recombination
breakpoints where edges begin and end. Between two breakpoints the local tree is
identical, so there is nothing to switch *against*; the ancestry is constant by
construction over the edge's span. The recombination structure that Relate / tsinfer
/ SINGER already inferred therefore *supplies* the horizontal coupling, and $\rho$
disappears as a free nuisance parameter.

### Why this decomposition is more falsifiable than a generic $\rho$-HMM

A generic ancestry HMM lumps *all* state change into a single switch rate $\rho$
estimated from the data. tspaint instead **decomposes** state change into two
mechanisms with separate, identifiable channels:

- **recombination** — horizontal, a *topology* change, handled entirely by edge
  spans (the inferred ARG);
- **ancestral migration** — vertical, handled entirely by $Q$.

Because the edge representation separates them cleanly, $Q$ estimates *only* the
ancestral-migration timescale and tract switching is handled *only* by the
topology. This pins each mechanism to an observable structure rather than to one
catch-all rate, which makes the model more specific — and therefore more
**falsifiable** — than a generic $\rho$-HMM: the recombination breakpoints are not
free to absorb migration, nor vice versa.

## The deliverable: a calibrated soft posterior {#sec-deliverable}

For each tip (haplotype) in each marginal tree — equivalently each tip-edge span —
the model's output is the **down-pass posterior** over the tip's ancestry,

$$
\gamma_i(s) = \Pr(s_i = s \mid \text{local tree}, \theta),
\qquad \theta = (Q, \pi, \{w_i\}),
$$ {#eq-posterior}

a *soft* probability, not a hard call. Because the local tree topology is shared
across a span and the per-edge accounting ties states together along the genome,
$\gamma_i$ is **spatially smoothed** without an explicit smoother. Two emergent
behaviours follow directly from @eq-posterior and the emission model:

- **Graceful relaxation.** When the local tree *cannot discriminate* — e.g. a query
  coalesces with both source clades at similar depth — $\gamma_i$ relaxes toward
  the prior $\pi$ (50/50 for a uniform two-state prior) rather than making a
  confident wrong call. This is a consequence of the generative model, not a
  bolted-on rule.
- **Missing information is distinct from uncertainty.** A tip that is *isolated*
  over an interval (no parent, no children) is a root unto itself: the tree says
  *nothing* about its ancestry there. Its posterior falls back to $\pi$, but the
  output marks this span as **missing-info**, distinct from a genuine 50/50
  *uncertain* call — a real interpretive distinction for introgression mapping.

A schematic of the emission likelihood vector and the resulting per-tip posterior:

```python
# Illustration only — see the API reference for the real functions.
import numpy as np
from tspaint.model import tip_emission, query_emission, transition_matrix

pi = np.array([0.5, 0.5])            # uniform root frequencies (A, B)

e_ref   = tip_emission(label=0, w=0.9, pi=pi)   # labelled A, credibility 0.9
e_query = query_emission(pi)                    # unlabelled query tip
P_t     = transition_matrix(Q, t)               # branch factor exp(Q t)
# Felsenstein pruning combines {e_i} and {P(t)} into the posterior gamma_i (eq-posterior).
```

## Modelling assumptions and their limits

The model is deliberately a **mugration** approximation: ancestry is treated as a
trait on a *fixed* inferred genealogy. This ignores that genealogy and ancestry are
jointly distributed (lineages in different populations cannot coalesce) — the
structured-coalescent point that BASTA and MASCOT correct, and that SCAR
([Guo et al. (2022)](https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/journal.pcbi.1010422))
addresses on inferred ARGs for a different deliverable (migration demography, not
per-haplotype painting). For a v1 painting tool the naive version is expected to be
adequate, but it **biases $Q$**, and the inferred branch lengths feeding
@eq-transition carry their own calibration assumptions; these limits are stated
plainly here so downstream interpretation accounts for them.

## Equation reference

For cross-referencing from other pages:

| Label | Object |
|:--|:--|
| `@eq-generator` | the 2-state generator $Q$ |
| `@eq-rowsum` | generator row-sum and non-negativity constraints |
| `@eq-transition` | branch transition $P(t) = \exp(Q t)$ |
| `@eq-emission` | soft tip emission with credibility $w_i$ |
| `@eq-query-emission` | query (flat / root-frequency) emission |
| `@eq-credibility` | MAP credibility update under the $\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)$ prior |
| `@eq-posterior` | the per-tip down-pass posterior $\gamma_i(s)$ (the deliverable) |
